perturbation.original_text: F. "Control" shall mean the direct or indirect ownership of over 50% of the capital stock (or other ownership interest, if not a corporation) of any entity or the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct the management and policies of such entity by ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise. "Controlling" shall mean having Control of any entity and "Controlled" shall mean being the subject of Control by another entity.
perturbation.changed_text: F. "Control" shall mean the direct or indirect ownership of over 25% of the capital stock (or other ownership interest, if not a corporation) of any entity or the possession, directly or indirectly, of the power to direct the management and policies of such entity by ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise. "Controlling" shall mean having Control of any entity and "Controlled" shall mean being the subject of Control by another entity.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the definition of 'Control' from 50% to 25% contradicts the commonly accepted and legally understood definition of control in corporate law, creating uncertainty about when control is actually established.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 2(a)(2) of the Bank Holding Company Act defines control as ownership or control of 25 per centum or more of any class of voting shares
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the Bank Holding Company Act's definition of control, which specifies the ownership or control of 25% or more of any class of voting shares. The original contract's 50% threshold aligns more closely with general corporate control definitions but is inapplicable in this context.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 12 / CHAPTER 17 / § 1841 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 12 USC 1841 : Definitions Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 12-BANKS AND BANKING CHAPTER 17-BANK HOLDING COMPANIES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous References In Text Amendments Effective Date Short Title §1841. Definitions (a)(1) Except as provided in paragraph (5) of this subsection, "bank holding company" means any company which has control over any bank or over any company that is or becomes a bank holding company by virtue of this chapter. (2) Any company has control over a bank or over any company if- (A) the company directly or indirectly or acting through one or more other persons owns, controls, or has power to vote 25 per centum or more of any class of voting securi
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the definition of 'Control' from 50% ownership to 25%. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts the Bank Holding Company Act's definition of control, which specifies 25% or more ownership. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 12 USC 1841, supports this by defining a bank holding company as any company that controls 25% or more of any class of voting securities. By lowering the threshold to 25%, the modified text introduces a definition that conflicts with the existing legal standard, potentially causing confusion and non-compliance, as it now aligns with the Bank Holding Company Act instead of general corporate control definitions as originally intended.

perturbation.original_text: K. "Damages" shall mean all direct, actual and verifiable losses, liabilities, damages and claims and related costs and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs, costs of investigation, litigation, settlement, judgment, interest and penalties) but excluding any and all consequential, incidental, punitive and exemplary damages.
perturbation.changed_text: K. "Damages" shall mean all losses, liabilities, damages and claims and related costs and expenses (including attorneys' fees and court costs, costs of investigation, litigation, settlement, judgment, interest and penalties), including consequential, incidental, punitive and exemplary damages.
perturbation.explanation: By including 'consequential, incidental, punitive and exemplary damages' in the definition of 'Damages', the contract now attempts to allow for types of damages that are often legally restricted or prohibited in commercial agreements, particularly in outsourcing contexts. This contradicts the standard practice of limiting damages to direct losses.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The general concept of limiting consequential damages is widely supported in contract law and is often upheld by courts.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the established principle under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) and common law, which allows parties to exclude consequential damages in contracts. By explicitly including them in the definition of 'Damages', the contract deviates from this standard legal practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-715. Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-715. Buyer's Incidental and Consequential Damages. (1) Incidental damages resulting from the seller's breach include expenses reasonably incurred in inspection, receipt , transportation and care and custody of goods rightfully rejected, any commercially reasonable charges, expenses or commissions in connection with effecting cover and any other reasonable expense incident to the delay or other breach. (2) Consequential damages resulting from the seller's breach include (a) any loss resulting from general or particular requirements and needs of which the seller at the time of contracting had reason to know and which could not reasonably be prevented by cover or otherwise; and (b) injury to person or property proximately resulting from any breach of warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Damages' to include 'consequential, incidental, punitive and exemplary damages,' which directly contradicts the standard practice of excluding such damages in commercial agreements. The Law Explanation states that parties are generally allowed to exclude consequential damages, which is undermined by including them in the definition of 'Damages'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'consequential damages resulting from the seller's breach,' implying that there are specific circumstances where they might be considered, however, the original agreement was set up to ensure they were not. This misalignment of terminology creates a legal contradiction as it deviates from legally recognized definitions and standard practices, making certain provisions of the contract potentially unenforceable. This falls under the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: X. "Term" shall mean the period commencing on the Effective Date and terminating on the eighth anniversary of the Commencement Date, unless the Agreement is extended in accordance with its provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: X. "Term" shall mean the period commencing on the Effective Date and terminating on the tenth anniversary of the Commencement Date, even if the Agreement is terminated early according to its provisions.
perturbation.explanation: By stating the term extends to the tenth anniversary even if terminated early, the modified text introduces a contradiction. Contract law generally allows for early termination clauses to supersede the original term, but this modification creates ambiguity about the actual duration and obligations upon early termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding termination clauses.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts basic contract law principles, specifically as outlined in the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, which emphasizes the importance of clearly defined termination clauses and their effects. The addition nullifies the termination clauses. A proper URL to Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 237 is not available as it is not codified law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by misaligning the term 'Term' with its statutory meaning, making the contract potentially unenforceable. The original text defines 'Term' as ending on the eighth anniversary unless extended. The changed text extends the term to the tenth anniversary even if terminated early, contradicting the effect of early termination clauses. This deviates from standard contract law principles, creating ambiguity about the actual duration. The Law Explanation mentions the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, highlighting the importance of clearly defined termination clauses. While scraped snippets do not explicitly support this, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, in conjunction with the definitions provided for the perturbation type, indicate that the modified clause introduces an ambiguity that undermines the contract's enforceability because it is unclear when it actually ends.
